title: DEPT: Decoupled Embeddings for Pre-training Language Models

reviews.summary: This paper proposes DEPT, a communication-efficient pre-training framework that decouples token and positional embeddings from the transformer body to better handle heterogeneous data sources (domains and languages). DEPT comprises three variants—GLOB (local-SGD/FedAvg with shared embeddings), TRIM (per-silo trimmed embeddings projected back to a global vocabulary), and SPEC (fully silo-specialized embeddings and positional encodings)—and aims to reduce negative interference, memory/communication costs, and vocabulary dilution while improving generalization and plasticity. Experiments on multi-domain (The Pile) and multilingual (mC4) mixtures with decoder-only models up to ~1.3B parameters show sizable perplexity and downstream gains, along with large reductions in communication and embedding memory, and demonstrate feasibility of vocabulary-agnostic federated pre-training at billion-parameter scale.
strengths: The core idea—to explicitly decouple (or partially decouple) embeddings from the shared transformer body across heterogeneous sources—is simple yet non-trivial, with clear architectural implications and practical benefits.

SPEC’s fully decoupled setup that supports per-silo vocabularies and positional embeddings without shared tokenization is novel in federated/heterogeneous pre-training contexts and addresses vocabulary dilution and tokenization mismatch head-on.
The TRIM variant’s projection mechanism with zero-padding and per-token aggregation is a neat, lightweight way to reduce memory/communication while maintaining a global vocabulary.

The paper evaluates across both multi-domain (16 The Pile subsets) and multilingual (8 mC4 languages, with OOD languages) scenarios, reporting perplexity, OOD performance, plasticity, and downstream NLU tasks.
Comparisons include multiple sampling baselines (STD at τ=0, 0.3, 1), and an active forgetting baseline (ACT) adapted to the setting.
The “continued pre-training” stage with random embedding initialization is a thoughtful diagnostic that isolates transformer body quality.
Results are shown at multiple model scales (125M–1.3B), and the authors discuss stability (activation/parameter norms), communicating training robustness.
The motivation—capacity contention, vocabulary dilution, and suboptimal tokenization under heterogeneity—is well articulated and connects to prior empirical findings.
The three variants are clearly contrasted in terms of communication/memory trade-offs and assumptions (shared vs. specialized vocabularies/positions).
The TRIM aggregation is formalized with indicator matrices; the communication/memory table succinctly conveys scaling implications.

Communication and memory savings are practically important for cross-silo and low-bandwidth pre-training; enabling vocabulary-agnostic federated pre-training at billion-parameter scale is a meaningful milestone.

The consistent generalization/plasticity gains suggest DEPT can be a useful first-stage pre-training recipe that complements data-centric strategies (e.g., filtering, mixture engineering) highlighted in recent literature.
Findings are relevant to multilingual and multi-domain LLM pre-training, where scalability, stability, and fairness to low-resource sources matter.
weaknesses: SPEC requires a post-hoc “continued pre-training” stage to build a usable global embedding; this dependency can add 15–19% extra steps and potentially alter comparisons to baselines that do not intrinsically need a second stage.

TRIM changes the training objective by restricting the output space locally during pre-training (given tied input-output embeddings), which may introduce subtle comparability concerns to STD that predicts over the full vocabulary at all times.
The algorithm pseudocode interleaves the three variants in a single loop and is confusing in its current form (lines 4–7 read as executed in sequence rather than mutually exclusive paths).

Many headline improvements rely on the “randomly reinitialized embeddings” evaluation to isolate transformer body quality; while justified, this setup downplays the role of embeddings in real deployments. The “pre-trained embedding” comparison for GLOB/TRIM shows smaller or mixed gains, indicating sensitivity to the continued pre-training mixture and sampling.

The multilingual suite is limited to eight languages (with three OOD languages); claims about alleviating the curse of multilinguality would be stronger with larger language coverage (e.g., 30–100+ as in recent studies).

Comparisons to other low-communication/distributed pre-training approaches (e.g., DiLoCo, DisTrO, Local SGD baselines beyond FedAvg, or MoE-style decoupling of capacity) are discussed but not empirically benchmarked.

Compute/wall-clock accounting and cost-equivalence across methods are under-specified; communication byte counts are helpful, but end-to-end training time and throughput are crucial for practical claims.

Some tables (e.g., Table 2) mix memory/comms units and relative factors in ways that are hard to parse at a glance; consistent units and a clearer definition of “per-step comms” versus “per-round” would help.
The OOD perplexity numbers are extremely large for some languages, suggesting substantial tokenization mismatch; framing and normalization could be improved to ease interpretation.

The paper could better position DEPT relative to strong, recent multilingual findings that attribute “curse” effects more to data quality and absolute token counts than to language count per se (e.g., 2510.25947): DEPT’s gains appear complementary but this nuance is not fully explored.

More direct connections to monolingual tokenizer advantages (Goldfish; 2408.10441) and massively multilingual continual pre-training (EMMA-500; 2409.17892) would clarify how DEPT bridges monolingual tokenization benefits with a shared body.
Empirical comparison to other vocabulary/embedding transfer approaches (e.g., trans-tokenization/vocabulary transfer, Remy et al., 2024) would strengthen the SPEC story.
reviews.questions:
1. How are TRIM’s per-token updates weighted during aggregation across silos to avoid overweighting frequent tokens or sources? Do you normalize by token counts or client data size?
2. Can you provide wall-clock time and throughput comparisons (end-to-end, including the continued pre-training phase for SPEC) to substantiate the practical efficiency claims, not just communication bytes?
3. How sensitive are the results to Nlocal (local steps) and client participation rate? Is there a stability or accuracy trade-off as Nlocal grows (as in Local SGD literature)?
4. Could you include an empirical comparison with DiLoCo/DisTrO-style low-communication training or a MoE baseline, to better position DEPT against state-of-practice communication-efficient or capacity-partitioning alternatives?
5. For SPEC, how critical is specializing positional embeddings versus only token embeddings? An ablation (SPEC without positional specialization) would clarify the incremental benefit.
6. The continued pre-training stage adds 15–19% extra steps: how do results change if this budget is reduced/increased? Is there a minimal budget at which SPEC/GLOB/TRIM maintain their advantage?
7. Multilingual experiments cover 8 languages (plus 3 OOD). Do your conclusions persist with 30–50+ languages (e.g., mC4 or FineWeb-2 subsets)? Any preliminary results or insights?
8. Can you release code/tokenizers and, if possible, trained checkpoints for at least the 125M/350M settings to aid reproducibility?
reviews.assessment: This paper tackles a timely and practically important challenge in multilingual and multi-domain pre-training: how to prevent embedding-level interference, reduce communication/memory costs, and improve robustness when training on heterogeneous corpora. The central idea—decoupling or partially decoupling embeddings from a shared transformer body—is simple yet promising, and the SPEC/TRIM design choices are well-motivated. Empirically, the authors present a convincing body of evidence that DEPT produces transformer bodies with better generalization and plasticity and can drastically reduce communication. The work is not without caveats: the strongest gains are measured in a “random-embedding” continued pre-training setup that emphasizes transformer-body quality rather than end-to-end readiness; SPEC requires a follow-up stage; and comparisons to alternative low-communication or capacity-partitioning methods are limited. Nonetheless, the contribution is significant and likely to be of interest to the ICLR community working on scalable, multilingual, and federated LLM pre-training. With additional clarifications (aggregation weighting, wall-clock reporting), expanded multilingual coverage, and a couple of stronger baselines, this paper would be a solid accept. As it stands, I lean toward acceptance due to the novelty, technical soundness, and compelling empirical findings.
